content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 27, 1991


Charles Wachob
City Attorney
City of Auburn
1225 Lincoln Way
Auburn, CA 95603

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-464

Dear Mr. Wachob:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Auburn City Councilmember Deborah Yue with respect to
her duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your request seeks
general guidance with respect to a variety of decisions, we are
providing informal assistance applicable to the decisions
generally.²

QUESTION

May Councilmember Yue participate in decisions concerning
improvement of the storm drainage system in the old town area of
Auburn?

CONCLUSION

     If the decision to improve the drainage in the old town area
will result in new or substantially improved services to the
councilmember's property, the councilmember may not participate in
the decision.


___________________________

     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

     ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 2


FACTS

The City of Auburn has had problems with flooding in the area
of the city occupied by old town. The old town area contains
historic buildings built in the 1850s. In 1986 the area flooded
due to a significant storm, and extensive damage to both private
and public property resulted. The area was declared a disaster
area by the state.

There are two principal drainages that enter the old town
commercial/residential area. The larger is the Lincoln Way
Drainage from the north and the smaller is the Brewery Lane
Drainage to the south. The confluence of the two drainages lies
beneth old town.

Approximately 1 square mile, or about 16 percent of the total
city, drains through the area. However, the existing system
cannot handle the runoff from a minor storm. The Lincoln Way
Basin can handle 150 cubic-feet per second (cfs) and the Brewery
Lane Basin 75 cfs. Peak flow through the basins from a major
storm could result in 570 cfs runoff into the Lincoln Way Basin
and 170 cfs into the Brewery Lane Basin. Approximately 600
residences are located in the Lincoln Way Basin and 125 in the
Brewery Lane Basin, representing a population of over 1,700.

Councilmember Yue is married to Herbert Yue. The Yues own
property and reside at 215 Sacramento Street. Herbert Yue also
owns, with Harry Yue, a parcel at 147 Brewery Lane in joint
tenancy.

Herbert Yue is also the president and 25-percent shareholder
of RHRH, Inc. RHRH, Inc. is a small closely held family
corporation. Herbert Yue receives a salary from RHRH, Inc.

RHRH, Inc. owns a parcel on Washington Street in the old town
area, which is leased by Century 21 for real estate offices. In
addition, RHRH, Inc. leases property at 289 Washington Street,
which is also in the old town area, at which RHRH, Inc. operates
the Shanghai Restaurant. The Shanghai Restaurant has sustained
flood damage in the past from major storms.

The city council has decided that improvement to the drainage
in the old town area is necessary. Councilmember Yue did not
participate in this decision. You have asked whether the
councilmember may participate in the following decisions:

1. Budgeting Auburn Urban Development funds for portions of
the corrective measures;

2. Consideration of a utility user fee to fund the
improvements;

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 3


3.     Contracts with municipal bond underwriters and a bond
counsel in connection with a possible bond issuance;

4.     Approval of designating city and Auburn Urban Development
funds for the improvements;

5.     Selection of and contracts with engineers to perform the
project.

ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.  Councilmember Yue is a public
official under the Act.  (Section 82048.)

     Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

* * *

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 4


spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

                                    Section 87103(a),(b) and (c)
                                    emphasis added.

    Presumably, Councilmember Yue's interest in the Sacramento
Street property at which the Yues reside is greater than $1,000.
(Section 87103(b).)  Moreover, the councilmember has an indirect
interest in the Brewery Lane property and RHRH, Inc., of a value
equivalent to the interest of her spouse. (Section 87103(b).)
Presumably Mr. Yue's one-half interest in Brewery Lane
property and his 25-percent interest in RHRH, Inc are both worth
more than $1,000. (Section 87103(a).)

    In addition, Mr. Yue has a variety of economic interests by
virtue of his ownership interest in RHRH, Inc. Pursuant to
Section 87103, set forth above, these would also be indirect
interests of the councilmember. For example, RHRH, Inc. owns
property on Washington Street. Section 82033 defines "interest in
real property" to include a pro-rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual
or a member of the official's immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.
Thus, Mr. Yue's 25 percentage interest in RHRH, Inc. equates to a
25 percent interest in the Washington Street property. If the
value of this interest in the property is greater than $1,000, the
councilmember will also have an indirect economic interest in the
Washington Street property.

    Moreover, RHRH, Inc leases property in the old town area on
which RHRH, Inc. operates the Shanghai Restaurant. Since it
appears the Shanghai Restaurant is a subsidiary business of RHRH,
Inc., Mr. Yue has a 25 percent interest in the restaurant3 and
consequently, Councilmember Yue also has a 25 percent indirect
interest in the Shanghai Restaurant.

    Finally, Century 21 is a source of income to RHRH, Inc., and
Mr. Yue owns 25 percent of RHRH, Inc. Thus, 25 percent of the
income from Century 21 is attributable to Mr. Yue. (Section
82030.) If the councilmember's community property interest in
this income (one-half of Mr. Yue's 25 percent portion of the
income) is $250 or more during the past 12 months, Century 21 will
also be a source of income to the councilmember.


_________________________
3    The "investments" of an individual includes a pro-rata share of
investments of any business entity in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-
percent interest or greater. (Section 82034; Regulation 18706;
Regulation 18236.)

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 5


     Thus, to summarize, the councilmember has economic interests
in the following: (1) the Sacramento Street property; (2) the
Brewery Lane property; (3) RHRH, Inc.; (4) the Washington Street
property; (5) Century 21; and, (6) the Shanghai Restaurant.
Consequently, the councilmember is prohibited from making or
participating in decisions which could have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of these economic
interests, if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally.

Foreseeability

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

     According to your facts, it appears that Councilmember Yue
has interests in a variety of properties that will be benefited by
the proposed improvement of the storm drainage system in that
region. Thus, it is substantially likely, if not certain, that
the decisions concerning the improvement of the flood drainage in
the area will have a financial effect on the councilmember's
property.

Materiality

     The Commission has adopted guidelines to determine whether a
financial effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. The test to determine materiality
differs depending on whether the property interest of the official
is directly or indirectly affected by the decision.

     It appears from your facts that the councilmember's property
will be indirectly involved in the decision. The councilmember's
real property is directly involved in a decision if the decision
involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale,
purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city,
county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of
councilmember's property; the issuance, denial or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of her property; the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on her
property; or redevelopment of the councilmember's property.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).) None of these categories apply in
this case.

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 6


Regulation 18702.3 sets forth standards for determining
materiality with respect to governmental decisions which
indirectly affect real property. Among other things, the indirect
effect of the decision on an official's real property is material
if:

(2) The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).

You stated that the councilmember's property will receive new
or substantially improved services as a result of the drainage
improvements in the area. Consequently, the decision to improve
the drainage in the area will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on the councilmember and the councilmember may
not participate in the decision.

Since it appears that the decisions will have a foreseeable
and material financial effect on the councilmember's real property
we have not analyzed their financial effect on the councilmember's
business interests. (See generally, Regulation 18702.2.)

"Public Generally" Exception

Public officials with economic interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the effect of their interests is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Regulation 18703.) The "public"
consists of the entire jurisdiction of the agency in question.
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) This is so because all the
residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of the official.

For the "public generally" exception to apply to your facts,
a decision must:

1. Affect a significant segment of the population of Auburn;
and,

2. Affect the segment in substantially the same manner as it
will affect the official's interests.

You have not provided specific numbers concerning the
population of Auburn and the number of persons in the proposed
drainage improvement area who are similarly situated as
Councilmember Yue. You stated that 1,700 persons lived in the

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 7


basin area. If we assumed that all those persons would be
affected in the same manner as the councilmember by the decision,
since the segment in the basin area constitutes 16 percent of the
10,615 population of the City of Auburn, the "public generally"
exception would apply.

However, it does not appear that the number of persons in the
area which will benefit from the flood drainage improvements are
affected in a manner substantially similar to Councilmember Yue.
Councilmember Yue has three parcels of real property, a source of
income and a business interest that could be greatly benefited by
the improvement to the drainage system.³ The number of persons
with such extensive economic interests in the area would appear to
be relatively small. Taking this factor into consideration, it
does not appear that a significant segment of the population of
Auburn will be affected in substantially the same manner as the
councilmember. Thus, the "public generally" exception would not
apply.

Segmentation of Decisions

You have asked whether the various decisions relating to the
drainage project may be separated so that the councilmember may
participate in future decisions concerning the improvements. In
some cases, decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately. For example, if the resolution of one decision will
effect the determination of a decision for which an official is
disqualified, the councilmember must disqualify himself as to
both. Thus, if any of the financing decisions set forth in your
letter could affect the project as a whole, the councilmember may
not participate.

However, we have advised in past letters that under some
circumstances, decisions implementing projects may be treated
separately. For example, in the Moe Advice Letter (No. A-89-454)
we advised that where an official has a conflict of interest with
respect to the formation of an assessment district due to
potential effects on a source of income, he may still participate
in implementing decisions. We stated:

If the decision is one which is critical to
the project and could result in its termination or
significant modification, Mr. Abbott must refrain
from participating in it to the extent that the


_________________________
⁴ It is not clear from your letter whether all of the interests
discussed will be affected by the improved flood drainage system.
Since all the property is in rather close proximity to one
another, we have assumed that the improved services will be
provided to all of the properties in question. If this is
incorrect, please contact us for further advice.

Our File No. I-91-464
Page 8


total effect of the project will materially affect
...[an economic interest]... according to the test
set forth above. If the decision is one which
implements a decision previously made, the effect
of the decision could be measured by assessing the
difference between various implementation
alternatives presented.

You stated that future decisions before the city council
include the selection of an engineer and bond underwriter. These
decisions appear to be merely implementing decisions which may be
separable from the project decisions as a whole. Thus, while the
project as a whole will have a material financial effect on the
councilmember, this effect may be disregarded and the
councilmember may participate in the implementing decisions if the
decisions will not foreseeably and materially affect the
official's economic interests.

Thus, where Councilmember Yue has a conflict of interest with
respect to the drainage plan as a whole, the councilmember may
participate in later implementation decisions so long as: (1) the
decision will not have a material financial effect on her,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, and (2)
the decision will not in any way affect the results of decisions
for which the councilmember was already disqualified. (Athan
Advice Letter, No. A-86-094.)

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁵

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


⁵ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission November 27, 1991 Charles Wachob City Attorney City of Auburn 1225 Lincoln Way Auburn, CA 95603 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-91-464 Dear Mr. Wachob: This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of Auburn City Councilmember Deborah Yue with respect to her duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your request seeks general guidance with respect to a variety of decisions, we are providing informal assistance applicable to the decisions generally.² QUESTION May Councilmember Yue participate in decisions concerning improvement of the storm drainage system in the old town area of Auburn? CONCLUSION If the decision to improve the drainage in the old town area will result in new or substantially improved services to the councilmember's property, the councilmember may not participate in the decision. ___________________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-91-464 Page 2 FACTS The City of Auburn has had problems with flooding in the area of the city occupied by old town. The old town area contains historic buildings built in the 1850s. In 1986 the area flooded due to a significant storm, and extensive damage to both private and public property resulted. The area was declared a disaster area by the state. There are two principal drainages that enter the old town commercial/residential area. The larger is the Lincoln Way Drainage from the north and the smaller is the Brewery Lane Drainage to the south. The confluence of the two drainages lies beneth old town. Approximately 1 square mile, or about 16 percent of the total city, drains through the area. However, the existing system cannot handle the runoff from a minor storm. The Lincoln Way Basin can handle 150 cubic-feet per second (cfs) and the Brewery Lane Basin 75 cfs. Peak flow through the basins from a major storm could result in 570 cfs runoff into the Lincoln Way Basin and 170 cfs into the Brewery Lane Basin. Approximately 600 residences are located in the Lincoln Way Basin and 125 in the Brewery Lane Basin, representing a population of over 1,700. Councilmember Yue is married to Herbert Yue. The Yues own property and reside at 215 Sacramento Street. Herbert Yue also owns, with Harry Yue, a parcel at 147 Brewery Lane in joint tenancy. Herbert Yue is also the president and 25-percent shareholder of RHRH, Inc. RHRH, Inc. is a small closely held family corporation. Herbert Yue receives a salary from RHRH, Inc. RHRH, Inc. owns
